card.intuition: A property where making the same request multiple times has the same effect as making it once, preventing accidental duplicate actions.
card.analogy: An elevator button: pressing it once calls the elevator. Pressing it 20 more times doesn't make the elevator come faster or send 20 elevators — the final result is exactly the same.
when_to_use: Payment processing (avoiding double charges on a slow network click)
Retrying failed API calls automatically (safe to retry if the endpoint is idempotent)
Designing background jobs that might run more than once (At-least-once delivery)
gotchas: GET, PUT, and DELETE are idempotent by default in REST. POST is NOT idempotent unless you implement a 'deduplication' key
Idempotency doesn't mean the server returns the EXACT same response, but that the server STATE doesn't change after the first time

steps.label: Request
steps.annotation: Client sends request with unique Idempotency-Key.

steps.label: Check
steps.annotation: Server checks Redis/DB: has this key been processed before?

steps.label: Already done
steps.annotation: Key found: return cached response immediately — don't re-process.

steps.label: Process
steps.annotation: New key: execute the operation normally.

steps.label: Store
steps.annotation: Store result keyed by Idempotency-Key. Retries return same response.

exercise.prompt: Identify which HTTP verbs are idempotent in standard REST design.
hints: Reading (GET) doesn't change anything, so it's idempotent
Creating (POST) usually adds a new record each time
Replacing (PUT) or Deleting (DELETE) results in the same state regardless of how many times you do it
exercise.test_code:
assert is_idempotent["GET"] == True
assert is_idempotent["POST"] == False
assert is_idempotent["PUT"] == True
print("PASS")
